pitch.summary: SAMPLE/DEMO FIXTURE — ledgerline is a fictional medium-sized TypeScript web service used as the reference analysis for the Repo Onboarding tool. It implements a double-entry, event-sourced ledger exposed over an HTTP API: money movements are recorded as immutable journal entries, account balances are derived by folding those events, and an outbox pattern publishes settlement events to downstream consumers. It is designed to demonstrate how a well-factored domain-driven service is structured, from HTTP boundary through application services to a pure domain core and a Postgres-backed persistence layer.
pitch.audience: Backend engineers joining a payments/fintech team who need to understand an event-sourced, double-entry ledger and its DDD-style layering before making changes.

techStack.name: TypeScript
techStack.role: The entire service is written in strict-mode TypeScript; domain invariants are encoded in the type system (branded Money and AccountId types).

techStack.name: Node.js
techStack.role: Runtime for the HTTP server, the outbox worker, and the CLI migration tool.

techStack.name: Fastify
techStack.role: HTTP framework at the API boundary; route schemas double as request validation via JSON Schema.

techStack.name: PostgreSQL
techStack.role: System of record: stores the append-only journal, the derived balance snapshots, and the transactional outbox table.

techStack.name: Drizzle ORM
techStack.role: Typed query builder and schema definitions; used for all reads/writes and to generate SQL migrations.

techStack.name: Zod
techStack.role: Validates and parses inbound command payloads in the application layer before they reach the domain.

techStack.name: Vitest
techStack.role: Unit and integration test runner; domain tests run in-memory, integration tests hit a disposable Postgres via Testcontainers.

techStack.name: Pino
techStack.role: Structured JSON logging with per-request correlation ids threaded through the application layer.

techStack.name: Docker Compose
techStack.role: Spins up Postgres and a fake downstream consumer for local development and integration tests.

techStack.name: OpenTelemetry
techStack.role: Traces requests from HTTP handler through domain command handling to database spans.

architecture.title: Layered, dependency-inverted design
architecture.body: ledgerline follows a strict hexagonal / ports-and-adapters layering. Dependencies point inward: `interfaces` (HTTP, CLI) depend on `application`, which depends on the pure `domain`, which depends on nothing. Infrastructure (Postgres repositories, the outbox publisher) implements ports declared by the application layer and is wired in at the composition root (`src/main.ts`). This keeps the domain free of framework and I/O concerns, so business rules are testable in isolation. When reading the code, always ask which layer a file belongs to before assuming what it may import.

diagram.title: Ports & adapters layering
diagram.source:
flowchart TD
  subgraph interfaces["interfaces (adapters-in)"]
    HTTP["HTTP API (Fastify)"]
    CLI["migrate CLI"]
  end
  subgraph application["application"]
    CMD["command handlers"]
    QRY["query handlers"]
    PORTS["ports (interfaces)"]
  end
  subgraph domain["domain (pure)"]
    LEDGER["Ledger aggregate"]
    ENTRY["JournalEntry"]
    MONEY["Money value object"]
  end
  subgraph infra["infrastructure (adapters-out)"]
    REPO["Postgres repositories"]
    OUTBOX["Outbox publisher"]
  end
  HTTP --> CMD
  HTTP --> QRY
  CLI --> REPO
  CMD --> LEDGER
  QRY --> PORTS
  CMD --> PORTS
  LEDGER --> ENTRY
  ENTRY --> MONEY
  REPO -.implements.-> PORTS
  OUTBOX -.implements.-> PORTS

architecture.title: Event sourcing and derived balances
architecture.body: The ledger never stores mutable balances as the source of truth. Every money movement appends immutable `JournalEntry` events to the `journal` table. An account's balance is the fold of all entries touching it. For performance, a `balance_snapshots` table caches the balance at a known event sequence; reads replay only the entries after the snapshot. This is why you will see both a write path (append entries) and a rebuild path (recompute snapshots). Newcomers frequently trip on this: mutating a balance directly is always a bug — you emit an entry instead.

diagram.title: Core persistence model
diagram.source:
erDiagram
  ACCOUNT ||--o{ JOURNAL_ENTRY : "debited/credited"
  ACCOUNT ||--o| BALANCE_SNAPSHOT : "cached at seq"
  TRANSFER ||--|{ JOURNAL_ENTRY : "produces 2+"
  JOURNAL_ENTRY ||--o| OUTBOX : "enqueues"
  ACCOUNT {
    uuid id PK
    string currency
    string status
  }
  JOURNAL_ENTRY {
    bigint seq PK
    uuid account_id FK
    uuid transfer_id FK
    bigint amount_minor
    string direction
    timestamptz created_at
  }
  BALANCE_SNAPSHOT {
    uuid account_id PK
    bigint at_seq
    bigint balance_minor
  }
  OUTBOX {
    uuid id PK
    string topic
    jsonb payload
    timestamptz published_at
  }

architecture.title: The transfer command lifecycle
architecture.body: A transfer is the canonical write. The HTTP handler parses the request, the `TransferFunds` command handler loads both accounts, the domain validates the invariant that debits equal credits and that no account goes below its allowed floor, then the handler persists the new entries and an outbox row in a single database transaction. The outbox worker later publishes the settlement event. Follow this path end-to-end — it touches every layer and is the fastest way to understand the whole service.

diagram.title: POST /transfers happy path
diagram.source:
sequenceDiagram
  actor Client
  participant API as Fastify handler
  participant H as TransferFunds handler
  participant D as Ledger domain
  participant Repo as Postgres repo
  participant OB as Outbox worker
  Client->>API: POST /transfers {from,to,amount}
  API->>H: TransferFundsCommand
  H->>Repo: load(from), load(to)
  Repo-->>H: accounts + snapshots
  H->>D: ledger.transfer(from,to,money)
  D-->>H: JournalEntry[] (balanced)
  H->>Repo: BEGIN → insert entries → insert outbox → COMMIT
  Repo-->>H: ok
  H-->>API: TransferResult
  API-->>Client: 201 Created
  OB->>Repo: poll unpublished outbox
  OB->>OB: publish settlement.v1
  OB->>Repo: mark published

architecture.title: Concurrency and idempotency
architecture.body: Every transfer carries a client-supplied `idempotencyKey`. The handler upserts it into an `idempotency_keys` table with a unique constraint inside the same transaction as the entries; a duplicate key short-circuits and returns the original result rather than double-posting. Optimistic concurrency is enforced with the account's `version` column: two concurrent transfers on the same account cause one to fail the version check and retry. Understanding these two mechanisms explains most of the defensive code in the application layer.

nodes.label: src/main.ts
nodes.description: Composition root: builds config, wires adapters to ports, starts the HTTP server and outbox worker.

nodes.label: http/server
nodes.description: Fastify instance, plugin registration, error mapping.

nodes.label: http/routes/transfers
nodes.description: POST /transfers and GET /transfers/:id route handlers.

nodes.label: http/routes/accounts
nodes.description: Account creation and balance query routes.

nodes.label: cli/migrate
nodes.description: Runs Drizzle migrations from the command line.

nodes.label: application/TransferFunds
nodes.description: Command handler orchestrating a transfer across load, domain, persist, outbox.

nodes.label: application/OpenAccount
nodes.description: Creates a new account with a starting currency and floor.

nodes.label: application/GetBalance
nodes.description: Query handler that folds entries over the latest snapshot.

nodes.label: application/ports
nodes.description: Interfaces the domain-facing layer depends on: LedgerRepository, OutboxPort, Clock, IdGenerator.

nodes.label: domain/Ledger
nodes.description: Aggregate enforcing double-entry and floor invariants; produces balanced JournalEntry[].

nodes.label: domain/JournalEntry
nodes.description: Immutable event value object representing one debit or credit.

nodes.label: domain/Money
nodes.description: Branded integer-minor-units value object; forbids float arithmetic and cross-currency ops.

nodes.label: domain/Account
nodes.description: Account entity with currency, floor, status, and optimistic version.

nodes.label: infrastructure/PgLedgerRepository
nodes.description: Implements LedgerRepository over Drizzle; handles snapshots and version checks.

nodes.label: infrastructure/OutboxPublisher
nodes.description: Polls the outbox table and publishes settlement events; implements OutboxPort.

nodes.label: infrastructure/db-schema
nodes.description: Drizzle table definitions for journal, accounts, snapshots, outbox, idempotency_keys.

nodes.label: config
nodes.description: Loads and validates environment configuration with Zod.

nodes.label: observability/telemetry
nodes.description: OpenTelemetry setup and Pino logger factory.

nodes.label: fastify
nodes.description: HTTP framework.

nodes.label: drizzle-orm
nodes.description: Typed SQL query builder and migrations.

nodes.label: zod
nodes.description: Runtime schema validation.

nodes.label: pino
nodes.description: Structured logging.

nodes.label: @opentelemetry/sdk-node
nodes.description: Distributed tracing SDK.

nodes.label: PostgreSQL
nodes.description: System-of-record database.

nodes.label: settlement consumer
nodes.description: Downstream service that receives published settlement.v1 events.

codebaseMap.purpose: The pure business core: aggregates, entities, and value objects encoding the double-entry ledger rules with zero I/O or framework dependencies.

keyFiles.note: Ledger aggregate; the transfer invariant lives here.
Branded Money value object in integer minor units.
Account entity with floor, status, and version.

codebaseMap.purpose: Use-case orchestration: command and query handlers that load state via ports, invoke the domain, and persist results transactionally. Declares the ports that infrastructure must implement.

keyFiles.note: The canonical write path; read this first.
Folds entries over the latest snapshot.

codebaseMap.purpose: The HTTP boundary built on Fastify: route definitions, request/response schemas, and error-to-status mapping. Thin — it only translates HTTP to commands/queries.

keyFiles.note: Maps domain errors to HTTP status codes.

codebaseMap.purpose: Postgres adapters implementing the application ports via Drizzle, including snapshot handling and optimistic-version checks.
Transactional outbox worker that polls unpublished rows and emits settlement events to downstream consumers, giving at-least-once delivery.
Drizzle-generated SQL migrations that define and evolve the journal, accounts, snapshots, outbox, and idempotency tables.
Vitest suites: fast in-memory domain unit tests plus integration tests that run against a throwaway Postgres via Testcontainers.

keyFiles.note: Exhaustive invariant tests for the aggregate.

codebaseMap.purpose: Cross-cutting logging and tracing: the Pino logger factory and OpenTelemetry SDK bootstrap, wired so correlation ids flow from HTTP into domain command handling.

tour.title: Read the money model first
tour.why: Money is the atom every other concept is built from; understanding that balances are integer minor units (never floats) and that cross-currency arithmetic is a type error prevents almost every misconception a newcomer forms about this ledger.
tour.notice: The branded type trick that makes a raw number unusable where a Money is required, and the deliberate absence of any division or float operation.

tour.title: The JournalEntry event
tour.why: Once Money is clear, the next building block is the immutable event that records a single debit or credit; everything downstream (balances, outbox, snapshots) is a projection of these, so it must be understood before any handler.
tour.notice: That entries are immutable and carry a direction plus a transfer id — there is no 'update balance' anywhere; state changes are always new entries.

tour.title: The Ledger aggregate and its invariant
tour.why: This is the heart of the domain: it is where the double-entry rule (debits equal credits) and the account floor rule are enforced. Reading it now, with Money and JournalEntry understood, shows how business rules are expressed as pure functions returning events.
tour.notice: That `transfer()` returns a balanced array of JournalEntry and throws typed domain errors — it performs no I/O and knows nothing about Postgres.

tour.title: The ports the application depends on
tour.why: Before reading the command handler, see the interfaces it programs against; this reveals the dependency inversion that keeps the domain pure and makes it obvious what infrastructure must provide.
tour.notice: That LedgerRepository and OutboxPort are plain interfaces here — the Postgres implementation is nowhere in sight, which is the whole point of the architecture.

tour.title: The TransferFunds command handler — the spine of the service
tour.why: This single file ties together every layer you have read: it validates input, loads accounts through ports, calls the domain, and persists entries plus an outbox row in one transaction. It is the fastest path to a whole-system mental model.
tour.notice: The single transactional boundary wrapping entry inserts, the outbox insert, and the idempotency-key upsert — and how the version check drives optimistic concurrency retries.

tour.title: The Postgres repository implementation
tour.why: Now that you know what the handler expects from a LedgerRepository, seeing the concrete implementation grounds the abstraction in real SQL, snapshots, and version columns.
tour.notice: How reads fold only the entries after the latest balance snapshot, and how the optimistic version is asserted in the UPDATE's WHERE clause.

tour.title: The HTTP route that starts it all
tour.why: With the write path understood inside-out, zoom back to the boundary to see how thin the HTTP layer is — it only parses the request into a command and maps domain errors to status codes.
tour.notice: That the route contains no business logic and that each typed domain error maps to a specific HTTP status via the error-mapper.

tour.title: The outbox publisher and delivery guarantees
tour.why: The transfer wrote an outbox row but did not deliver it; this final step closes the loop and explains the at-least-once delivery guarantee that downstream consumers rely on.
tour.notice: The publish-then-mark ordering that makes redelivery (not loss) the failure mode, and why consumers must be idempotent as a result.

entries.insight: The most-edited file in the repo: it is where new transfer rules, idempotency, and concurrency handling accreted. High churn here means it is both central and still evolving — read it carefully and expect follow-up PRs to touch it.

Frequent changes track the maturing of business invariants (floors, currency rules). Because it is pure, changes here are usually accompanied by domain tests — a good place to see how rules are specified.
Churn spikes correlate with performance work on snapshots and version checks. If you hit a balance-read bug, history shows this file is the usual suspect.
Schema evolution shows the data model is not fully settled; each change here pairs with a migration, so treat the two as a unit.
Repeated fixes around delivery ordering and retry backoff — the comments and history here encode hard-won lessons about at-least-once semantics.
Mostly API-shape churn (validation, error responses). Lower risk than the domain, but a good indicator of how the public contract has changed.
Rarely touched and stable — a safe file to read to learn what the service needs from its environment without worrying it will shift under you.

hotspots.interpretation: Churn is concentrated in the write path (transfer handler, ledger aggregate, and Postgres repository), which confirms those three files are both the architectural core and the current locus of active development. A newcomer should invest reading time there and treat the low-churn config and domain value objects as stable ground to stand on.

prerequisites: Docker Desktop (for the local Postgres and integration tests)
A POSIX-ish shell (bash/zsh); Windows users should use WSL2 or Git Bash

setup.title: Install dependencies
setup.notes: Use npm ci for a clean, lockfile-exact install; npm install is fine for day-to-day work.

setup.title: Start Postgres and the fake consumer
setup.notes: Brings up Postgres on localhost:5432 and a stub settlement consumer on :4001.

setup.title: Configure environment
setup.notes: Defaults point at the docker-compose Postgres; no edits are needed for local dev.

setup.title: Run database migrations
setup.notes: Applies everything in migrations/ via the migrate CLI (src/interfaces/cli/migrate.ts).

run.title: Start the API in watch mode
run.notes: Runs the Fastify server and the outbox worker with hot reload; API listens on http://localhost:3000.

run.title: Smoke-test a transfer
run.notes: amount is in minor units (1000 = $10.00). Substitute the account ids returned by the first call.

test.title: Run fast domain unit tests
test.notes: Pure, in-memory, sub-second; no Docker needed.

test.title: Run integration tests
test.notes: Spins up a disposable Postgres via Testcontainers; requires Docker to be running.

test.title: Full check (types, lint, all tests)
test.notes: What CI runs: tsc --noEmit, eslint, and the complete Vitest suite.

knownRisks.title: Ledger writes and outbox delivery must stay atomic
knownRisks.impact: Committing a ledger entry without its matching outbox record can leave downstream projections permanently behind the source of truth.
knownRisks.mitigation: Keep both writes inside the Postgres transaction and exercise the integration tests that simulate a publish failure.

knownRisks.title: Money values cannot cross the domain boundary as floats
knownRisks.impact: Binary floating-point rounding can violate double-entry balance invariants and produce totals that cannot be reconciled later.
knownRisks.mitigation: Construct Money from integer minor units and keep transport parsing at the HTTP adapter boundary.

knownRisks.title: Projection updates are deliberately eventually consistent
knownRisks.impact: A successful transfer may be visible in the journal before every read model catches up, which can look like a lost update in manual testing.
knownRisks.mitigation: Assert on the ledger result first, then wait for the outbox relay when testing projection-facing behavior.

changeRoutes.changeType: Add or change a ledger business rule
changeRoutes.rationale: Ledger invariants belong in domain aggregates and value objects; application handlers should only coordinate those decisions.
verification: Add invariant-focused unit cases before adapting transport behavior

changeRoutes.rationale: HTTP parsing and response mapping are inbound-adapter concerns, while reusable use-case behavior stays in the application layer.
verification: Cover invalid transport input and the successful response contract

changeRoutes.rationale: Repository adapters own SQL and transaction boundaries, with schema evolution captured separately in ordered migrations.
verification: Run migrations against a clean database
Run npm run test:int with Docker available
Check rollback and outbox atomicity paths

firstTasks.title: Add a GET /accounts/:id/history endpoint
firstTasks.description: Expose the raw journal entries for an account as a paginated read model, reusing the existing GetBalance query plumbing to fetch entries.
firstTasks.rationale: It is read-only and cannot corrupt the ledger, yet it walks the newcomer through the full query path from HTTP to Postgres, cementing the layering.

firstTasks.title: Support a per-account overdraft floor
firstTasks.description: Let OpenAccount accept an optional negative floor and enforce it in the Ledger aggregate so approved accounts may go negative down to a limit.
firstTasks.rationale: It is a genuine domain rule change scoped to the pure core, so it teaches how invariants and their unit tests are written without touching infrastructure.

firstTasks.title: Add exponential backoff to the outbox publisher
firstTasks.description: Replace the fixed poll interval on publish failures with capped exponential backoff and jitter, and add a metric for retry counts.
firstTasks.rationale: It exposes the at-least-once delivery model and the observability wiring while staying contained to one adapter, a realistic reliability task.

firstTasks.title: Introduce a multi-currency transfer guard
firstTasks.description: Reject transfers between accounts of different currencies at the domain boundary with a typed error, and map it to HTTP 422.
firstTasks.rationale: It spans domain, value objects, and the HTTP error contract, forcing the newcomer to touch every layer and understand how errors propagate outward — a strong capstone onboarding task.
